title: End of module: The Wiener–Khinchin Theorem and Power Spectral Density

questions.text: The Wiener–Khinchin theorem states that, for a WSS process:
choices: The power spectral density is the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function
The autocorrelation function equals the process's mean squared
The PSD is always exactly flat regardless of the process
questions.explanation: S_X(f) = ∫R_X(τ)e^{-j2πfτ}dτ — an exact identity connecting the time-domain autocorrelation to the frequency-domain power spectral density.

questions.text: If ∫S_X(f)df = 40 for some WSS process, what is R_X(0)?
questions.explanation: Setting τ = 0 in the inverse Fourier transform relationship gives R_X(0) = ∫S_X(f)df directly — total area under the PSD equals average power.

questions.text: Since R_X(τ) is real and even, what does that imply about S_X(f)?
choices: S_X(f) is also real and even
Nothing can be inferred about S_X(f)
questions.explanation: The Fourier transform of a real, even function is itself real and even — exactly what a genuine PSD should look like.

questions.text: Why must S_X(f) be non-negative at every frequency?
choices: It represents power density, and power (like variance) can never be negative
There is no such requirement — S_X(f) can be any real number
questions.explanation: S_X(f) represents how much power sits in an infinitesimal band around frequency f; a negative value would claim negative power, which is physically meaningless — the same reasoning that makes variance and quadratic forms of covariance matrices non-negative.

questions.text: White noise with flat PSD S_X(f) = 4 passes through a filter with |H(f₀)|² = 3 at some frequency f₀. What is S_Y(f₀)?

choices: R_X(τ) = N₀δ(τ) — zero for any nonzero lag, since its PSD is perfectly flat
questions.explanation: The inverse Fourier transform of a constant (flat PSD) is a scaled impulse — white noise samples carry no information about any other sample at any nonzero lag.

questions.text: Cross-correlation R_XY(τ) between two related signals typically peaks at:
choices: The true time delay between the two signals, if one is a delayed copy of the other
Always exactly τ = 0, regardless of any delay
A value that depends only on the signals' means, not their timing
questions.explanation: If Y is a delayed copy of X by delay d, shifting Y back by τ = d realigns it with X, producing the strongest correlation at that specific lag — the basis of cross-correlation-based delay estimation.

questions.text: R_XY(τ) values are: τ=0: 3, τ=1: 7, τ=2: 12, τ=3: 5. What is the estimated time delay (the τ where the peak occurs)?
questions.explanation: The peak value (12) occurs at τ = 2, so that's the estimated delay between the two signals.

questions.text: One phrase (two words): the Fourier transform partner of cross-correlation, generalizing the PSD to a pair of related signals, is called the cross-spectral ___.
questions.explanation: The cross-spectral density S_XY(f) is the Fourier transform of R_XY(τ), the direct two-signal generalization of the PSD/autocorrelation pair.

questions.text: Cumulative review: from stationary-random-processes, why is it valid to estimate a WSS process's autocorrelation from a single finite recording at all?
choices: Because ergodicity, when it holds, guarantees a single realization's time-averaged statistics converge to the true ensemble statistics
Because every WSS process is automatically ergodic with no further assumption needed
Because autocorrelation doesn't require any assumptions about the process at all
questions.explanation: From stationary-random-processes: ergodicity (not stationarity alone) is the property that justifies estimating ensemble statistics — including R_X(τ) and, by Wiener–Khinchin, S_X(f) — from a single realization's time averages, which is exactly what the finite-data checkpoint in this module relied on.
